sentences.text: Yes.
So another miracle.
Oh my gosh, a life full of miracles.
The steps-- this time they're getting ready to march off.
And my heart was still going.
I don't know what my pulse was.
But it was beyond thinking then because this is now instinct-- the animal instinct of hiding.
You know, the hunted animal takes over where to hide from the hunter's rifle.
So the steps were leading someplace.
And I guess that's what he meant, upstairs, the guy who show me from a floor below.
And as I'm ready to climb the third set of steps, I hear some noise downstairs.
And I hear talking.
So I freeze in the hallway.
And I hear they were speaking Ukrainian-- Russian, and Ukrainian.
And there were two other guys, but they were running fast.
They were really fast.
And you could hear with the wooden shoes, they were clattering-- the clattering sound.
And they coming up to the first floor, the second floor.
And I'm trying to get up.
By the time I got up to the ceiling, it was like a trap door.
They were already behind me.
And they says, get out of here.
And one guy pulled my leg.
And I, like, fell down.
I said, wait a minute.
I was here first.
And they say, shut up.
And they run up through the attic quickly.
And one helped the other-- boof-- pushed him up.
And I'm trying to get up there myself.
And they-- [SMACK] shut the door at me-- the trap door.
And I couldn't get up in the attic.
And all of a sudden, I hear, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And somebody else says, moment, moment.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] One guy saw somebody running into the house.
So here is somebody-- the windows were open, of course.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] A flashlight, flashlight.
Somebody give me a flashlight.
He took over the searchlight.
And they're coming in.
And I hear their boots on the steps.
And I say, oh gosh.
And here I am, between the two floors.
And so now they're going to catch me.
And I can't get up to there.
They already shut the trap door.
So and the door to the left and the door to the right, nobody opens the door.
So I run quick down.
And I figure that I go begging for mercy.
I'll tell them I came for water and-- but maybe they won't believe me anyhow.
And so the sec-- I come back to the landing.
It was a little landing and a window going out to the front street and steps going down to the first floor from between the second and the first, in between landing-- between two floors.
On that landing next to the window was a sack-- looked like a potato sack.
But they were potato peelings.
In those days, the Germans even would save-- they didn't have enough potatoes themselves.
They would save the peelings.
They would then boil them and mash them and sprinkle them with corn and feed them to the chickens.
So the order was in those days not to throw away peelings.
Save your potato peelings.
So they would save a potato peelings in an apartment house like that.
And it was about 3/4 full.
And I must've weighed maybe 75, 80 pounds and was little anyhow.
So instinct again to-- the doors were locked either side of me.
This guy wouldn't let me in.
He keep saying, Jesus, Maria, you know.
So I quickly took the bag and emptied it of potato peelings and put my feet into the sack and pulled it over my face in a crouched position.
And the potato sack was in a corner of the hallway.
So my back was, of course, bare because I couldn't cover my back.
But I was in a corner.
Therefore, the walls covered my back.
But my face and part of my head were really covered.
And I crouch real tight.
And I was little, maybe weighed 75, 80 pounds.
And I sat in that position.
And this moment, until my dying day, I will never forget how long that particular moment was when I was in a potato sack.
I hear the boots coming up the steps.
Not in a real slow.
They were really running.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
I can hear the shouts vividly.
Today, I mean, when I talk about it, I can just see them, you know?
And they kicked the door.
Open.
Open.
And man opens up.
And he didn't talk German.
And they say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
Did you hide anybody-- and with the flashlight-- where?
Under the bed?
He says, no, no, no.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] No, no, no, no.
Next door, the same thing.
And I'm sitting there in this corner, in between the two floors on that landing.
They rush by me.
And I say, oh my  God, they ran by.
They were going up.
They go up to second floor.
They go up the third to the attic.
And I think about those two guys in the attic.
And the guy says, let's go up there.
And I hear them go.
And they push-- open the door.
And as they open the door, it fell.
You could hear the trap door wasn't hinged or-- I mean, when they opened the door, it fell on the floor, like, you know.
And I could hear it start to come down.
And the next thing I hear, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means don't shoot.
Don't shoot.
Don't shoot.
And the Germans shouting, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Boomp-- three or four shots.
And I say, oh my God, this is it.
Now and then I hear them talking.
Was it two of them or three?
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
And the other says, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
It's late.
I don't believe so.
I think that was it.
So this guy knocks on another door and goes, another one here?
And now they're coming down from the second floor.
They're coming close to where we are at that landing-- two guys.
The first guy went by me.
Then the-- and if you know what, potato sack is not really tight.
I could see.
And it's dark.
Potato sack has little holes, as you know, like an onion sack.
It's not woven really right.
And I could see through the holes.
And I could see the beam of the flashlight.
And as it came into my light, and it blinded me.
And I remember saying prayers.
I said my Shema.
I said, Sh'ma Yisrael.
As vividly as I'm talking to you now, I remember saying it.
And I figured, I just hope it's not going to hurt.
I figured if they shoot me, maybe it's in the heart or something, where it wouldn't hurt.
I shouldn't suffer.
And as he walked by, and I'm in this curved position, we're talking about a stairway, perhaps, of 36, 42 inches, his coat-- his little coat brushed my knees-- brushed by my knees.
If his boot, it would touch me or touch my wooden shoes, he would've discovered me.
But I felt his coat brushing by my knees.
And I said, this is it now.
But nothing happens.
They keep going-- going down, down there, another step, another step.
And I can't believe.
And I blink, and I touch myself.
Oh, no shots didn't.
So I hear them downstairs-- they're right below me.
He say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]
And they're marching away.
And I hear marches.
And I say, oh.
And I'm speaking to God now, really.
I'm really close to God because this is-- I really saw his work now.
I mean, this is a miracle.
This is crossing the Mediterranean.
The sea has parted for me right at this moment, you know?
And my heart is, I'm sure, beating fast and loud.
And I'm sitting there deciding what to do next.
No, I can't go out.
I better wait.
I better wait.
And I hear the steps going farther and farther away, farther away, farther away.
And I sit there for two reasons.
One, I don't want to come out too early.
And then I didn't even know what to do, where to go.
And that's where my odyssey of hiding began, when I went out of the sack and went in to the guy who threw me the pants.
And again, he wanted to faint.
He again crossed himself.
And he said, he has three children.
He cannot hide me.
And spoke good Polish.
And in my pure Polish, I told him, after all, I'm your brother.
How can you not help me?
So he sends me to another Polish guy who lives down the road.
And then he gives me a jacket for about four or five sizes too big.
But I was grateful for it.
So I throw my old prison jacket away.
And my odyssey of hiding out began and this lasted-- I thought, as I told you, the reason I made a getaway because I couldn't march any longer.
And after all, next day, the allies will be here anyhow.
So I figured I probably don't need a jacket anyhow.
I'll be liberated.
Little did I know it would take until the 5th of May, which is a month from that day I made my get away before the allies came.
As I said, there was a continued odyssey of hiding out in the barns.
This Polish guy sent me to another guy, who gave me some food.
And he took me to a barn just on the outskirt where his farm-- you see, they were civilian workers.
And they worked on German for German farmers.
But they had no food.
They just worked.
They were like slave labor.
But there was much better than camps.
So they knew that this particular farmer had another barn where he was storing-- what, I believe hay or whatever.
And so he says, you can stay there.
And I'll bring you some food every evening.
And so he did for about four or five days, until one evening he came with another Polish guy.
And this Polish guy who throw me those pants.
He says, this is Stefano, yeah?
Or whatever his name was-- [NON-ENGLISH].
This is-- I never told him my real name.
I told him Yusef or whatever-- whatever your name-- and he says, I'm helping him.
So this other Polish guy, who my-- I said, my Polish guy-- the one that helped me with the pants and brought me the food, he introduce this new guy.
But the new guy looks at me.
And he said, I thought you're saving a Polish national.
You're saving a damn Jew.
And he use very bad language.
He says, can't you see he's a Jew?
And I say, who?
And I told you I speak street Polish, with some cuss words and something.
And I dig into my lexicon to whatever I could find to really-- and so he says, no, he's not Jewish.
So there's this new guy.
He says, well, he doesn't talk like a Jew.
But that doesn't mean he's not Jewish.
He says, listen, he says, you want to pull his pants down?
And by this time, I knew the truth will come out.
And I, out of desperation, I said, you know, who gave you the right?
You're the only one that's going to survive here by yourself?
The war is over.
And tomorrow we could be liberated.
And you're telling me I'm not Polish because I'm Jewish because you're going to pull my pants down?
And I really-- whatever I said, I put some cuss words in it.
And he says, you know, you talk smart.
I'll tell you what.
I'll give you a break at daytime.
But by evening-- this was in the morning-- by evening, if you're still here, I'll bring the Gestapo.
And he says, so this place better be empty.
So naturally, I was grateful that he gave me at least a chance to get away.
So I got up.
I knew this was too dangerous to stay.
And I start marching.
And I came to the next village.
I wanted to actually go through another town.
But the roads were blocked by then and Luftwaffe was shooting up to some aircraft.
So I had to turn around and took a side road and went up during an air raid, running with other people into a shelter because I didn't have any prison uniform on by then.
I had some oversized clothes.
Looked like a gang member today.
But anyway, and in this shelter, I heard somebody talk Russian.
I start talking to him.
I knew pretty good Russian by then.
And it turn out he was a Belarusian working also slave labor.
And when he ask me what I'm doing here, and I told him a story that I was in a transport and got bumped out.
And I was afraid to see so he wouldn't denounce me.
He says, no, don't worry.
We'll help you.
But I was leery.
So he says, we're working.
He says, my cousins and I, we're working for this farmer here.
He says, you can come with us.
And you have a place to stay?
I said, no.
He says, you can come with us.
And I figured, should I or shouldn't I?
But I grab any chance I could get.
So they took me with them.
And it turned out to be they were my life-savers, this family.
They would turn out to be-- Belarusian family-- non-Communist.
They volunteer to be in Germany because they belong to a certain movement in Russia.
They were anti-Communist.
And they spend the war and work in Germany.
And they felt very much sorry for me, how skinny I was.
So they got me in to introduce me to their boss, who got me papers.
They told him I'm a cousin.
Came from Bratslav, from Breslau.
We was all bummed out.
I lost the papers.
And Sergei-- they gave me a name, Sergei.
And I'll be working with them.
And so I put down my name.
I even got ration cards in German, which were a blessing then, but which almost turn out to be my downfall after liberation.
And I get to that in a minute.
With those people I spend, and I got to like it very much.
It was a whole family, the Belarusians-- like a patriarch-- a grandfather, with two sons, a daughter, son-in-laws, cousin.
And he was sort of the patriarch of the family.
In the evenings, he would sit down, tell stories, talk about the Russia and all that.
But the last few evenings, when the front was beginning to near, he was talking in a whisper to the rest of your family members.
He's afraid of the Russians advancing.
And they kept saying, no, the Americans are coming because he didn't want to be liberated by the Russian troops because of his anti-Russian sentiments.
And he was afraid he'd be sent to Siberia.
And I forget the name-- the date in May-- must've been 3rd, 4th, or something like that, the town's police chief already left.
And the SS, the police were already run away.
So that evening, they got together.
He kept talking in Russian.
He says, we have to run away because I heard that the Russians are coming to our town.
On the other side of the Albania, Americans already there.
But the Russians came that night, before he could run away.
And I was going to go with them.
They were going to take me with them.
And the Russian liberated us.
And I'd like to tell you a small detail, which is very important, also-- again, another little miracle.
And this was a miracle at liberation.
You remember I told you about my false identity.
They gave me papers.
And they introduced me to their boss, to the farmer, who went to the burgermeister, to the mayor of town and told him he hired one more guy from Eastern Germany.
His name is Sergei something.
I don't remember the last name.
And he got me ration cards-- an identity.
And was no pictures, but it was a fingerprint.
And I felt, gee, I'm liberated now.
I was working on a farm.
And it was like a civilian, which was great.
I gained weight, and, you know.
But four weeks later, when liberation came-- when the Russians came, that was almost my downfall.
They surrounded the little village.
They came in with the tanks, told everybody to assemble.
And my friends, the Russian-- the Belarusian family-- they must have been maybe a group of 12 or 14 people-- and we were all herded together into a truck, taken to the schoolyard.
We come to a big schoolyard.
This was already daylight.
And people are sitting there in groups.
And they escorting individually inside the schoolhouse to certain officers and identifying people-- who they are.
What country?
What nationality?
You got some papers?
My turn-- they ask me, where you from?
I tell them, Poland.
He says, you stay here.
They ask the other people where they from, and they are Russia.
They say, you go over here.
They separated us.
I never saw them again.
And I have no idea what happened to them.
But what almost happened to me, I can imagine what happened to them because if it wouldn't be for this little miracle-- I'll tell you the detail.
My turn came in to go to be identified and segregated.
Where you from?
How old are you?
What's your name?
I told them my  real name then.
But the paper says Sergei.
I say, yeah, but this is just a lie I told for the Germans because I run away from the concentration camp.
What?
Why were you in concentration camp?
Because I'm Jewish.
Oh yeah?
OK.
Where are you from Poland?
Told them my town, Siemianowice, was close to Katowice.
Your mom's name?
Your father's name?
He starts writing, hardly looking at me.
And he finished everything.
He says, so you're from Poland?
So what were you doing here?
I says, I was in the concentration camp.
See, but you were on a farm.
You had a different name.
How am I supposed to believe you?
You don't look like you came from concentration-- by this time, I gained weight.
And my hair started coming back.
And I says, well, I'm telling you the truth.
So he says, just a minute.
So he goes into the main office-- another office.
And he comes out.
And he says, OK, next.
He calls next.
He says, you sit here.
And he put me in a different chair.
And I'm sitting there waiting.
In a few minutes, the guy from the other office come out.
And the guy says, in.
So I'm stepping in another office.
And I see another guy sitting behind a big desk, smoking a cigarette, a Russian uniform with the epaulettes-- gold and some kind-- and I didn't know what his rank was.
But must've been a high.
Has some stars or this-- saw some stars.
And kind of burly looking guy, kind of heavyset, crew cut, mustache.
And looks at me.
He says, what's your name?
And I tell him.
Yeah.
And he looks at my papers.
That's not what it says here.
Again, the same thing.
How come you're Sergei and your name is Henry?
I says, yeah, because I am.
He say, why were you in concentration camp?
I say, well, that's what he asked me.
He says, I'm asking you now.
I says, well, because I'm Jewish.
Oh yeah?
What was your father's name?
Abraham.
What was your mother's name?
I say [NON-ENGLISH].
So what-- are you some kind of a spy?
You've got the two identities here.
I says, no, I'm not a spy.
I run away.
This guy gave me a pants-- suit.
This is what this-- these are not my pants.
See how big the jacket is?
The other guy gave me a jacket.
And I tell him the whole-- so he says, you know, I don't know if I should believe you.
We have to be very carefully.
We have a lot of enemies here.
So you better-- he leaves the desk, and he comes over to me, close to me, looks at me, faces me.
And he says, so you're Jewish, huh?
I says, yes, I am.
And you were in concentration camp?
I says, yes, I was.
He says, OK, if you're Jewish, finish this for me.
And he comes close to me and puts his hand on my shoulder like this.
He says, Sh'ma Yisrael [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Sh'ma Yisrael Adonai Eloheinu.
Finish it.
And I says, Sh'ma Yisrael Adonai Eloheinu Adonai Ehad.
[SPEAKING HEBREW] I couldn't finish.
He grabs me.
He embraces me, and we both cry.
This burly man-- 6 feet tall, maybe 200 some pounds-- tears are running down his cheek.
He hugs me, almost broke my bones.
He says, don't worry.
You'll be OK.
And he calls the-- [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]-- calls up the corporal.
Get him something to wear.
Give him some pants, boots, jacket.
Food-- give him some food.
And he reaches down, and he says, here, and give me some kind of a canned meat or something.
And he says, where are you going now?
And I'm so shook up.
And I say, oh, thank you so much.
He says, no, no.
Don't worry.
You'll be OK.
You want to go home to Poland, huh?
I say, yeah, sure.
He says, anybody left?
I say, I don't know.
I have a brother, a mother, and a father.
I don't know who is left.
He says, go back home.
Go back home.
I says, I'm too weak to walk.
And he says, don't worry.
We'll get you on a train.
So he goes back to his desk and starts writing.
He said, what  did you say your name was-- your real name.
I says, yeah, my real name.
So he got my real name and put my fingerprints on it.
And he makes me out a ID card with my real name, where I was born, raised, everything-- what camps I was in.
And it's in four languages.
And that ID got me for the next five days of traveling.
And it got me back to Poland on a train, a bus, a walk.
One Russian confiscated a bicycle.
I took a bicycle to the next town.
And because of this must have been colonel or this Russian officer who turn out to be Jewish, he saved my life.
Who knows if it was-- who knows, a non-Jew who would never believe me.
Oh, I forgot to tell you.
He said, I'm not going to pull your pants down because chances are the Nazis did it to you.
And this is why you asked me to recite the Shema.
But he say, I'm not going to pull your pants down.
So I thought it was a real human being.

And to him, I owe a lot of gratitude because of who knows, I probably would've wind up with the Russians-- with the Belarusians in Siberia as a traitor, after suffering all this time through ghettos and the camps after liberation.
So there, again, was another miracle.
And of course, that got me back to Poland.
And I came back to my town of Siemianowice in about five days of traveling-- open cars, boxcars, trains, bus.
And I had no money.
But I would stop.
And people from Red Cross would give me some meals and some traveling coupons.
Well, somehow, we got that.
It wasn't like today, you need traveler's checks.
So anyway, and I got back to Poland.
And I came to my town.
The first thing I went to, I went to my neighborhood, to the coal miners-- the apartment house.
And of course, somebody else was living there already.
And I knocked on the door on a neighbor who my mom was friendly with.
And she recognized me.
And she says, oh Jesus, Maria, [SPEAKING POLISH].
You alive!
Oh my!
Please come in.
And she fed me.
And of course, she found out I had no place to stay.
She gave me a place and asked me to stay with her.
And I spend the night with them.
And she gave me clean shirts from her husband.
And I kept asking about if anyone is alive.
And she says, you know, they said, I think maybe it's your brother or your cousin because my brother was named Max.
And I had my cousins.
And I also had a cousin on my father's side, also-- from my father's family.
And his name was also Max and the same last name.
And she says, I believe Max-- one of the Maxes is alive, but I don't know which one.
And I was asking, which one is it-- the heavy guy?
The chubby?
Max, my brother, was chubby when he was a kid.
He says, I think so.
But he says, he wasn't chubby this time.
So I still didn't know.
So I figured, I'll go to the innkeeper my father was friendly with and my folks.
So I went into the market square.
I went to the innkeeper.
And I thought, I'll ask him because during the war, we would have contact with him.
He would bring food to the ghetto.
We would buy some food from him and trade off some gold rings or whatever.
And I figured this would be a good place to get information.
I'm walking into the inn.
And a typical Polish inn-- two, three guys standing at the bar, smoke filled.
And some guys are sitting there drinking beer or sodas or whatever.
And they're talking loud and all that.
And the bartender-- his name was-- I still remember his name, until recently; it'll come to me-- stays there and wipes off some glasses and puts some beer in.
He was facing the door.
And there's two or three guys standing with their backs towards the door, facing the bartender.
And I hear him say something like, no, no, I didn't see him.
I don't know.
And as he talks, all of a sudden, he sees me at the door.
He freezes, and he quits speaking.
And his mouth drops open.
And he goes like this.
And I'm looking around.
I said, maybe somebody's behind me.
And he goes like this, pointing at me.
And there was some rumors that I got shot.
And I figured, well, he probably doesn't know I'm alive.
So I say-- gosh, what was his name?
Anyway, not Dombrowski.
I know, not-- anyway.
And I'm walking over.
And I says, [SPEAKING POLISH].
And he keeps pointing at me.
So one of the guys he's talking to turns around.
And I thought, I'm going to faint.
It was my brother standing there, asking if he didn't see-- if he knows anything about Henry.
And he sees me And oh, the reunion-- the screaming and the crying.
And people-- these two guys were drinking, sitting drinking beer.
And all of a sudden, they began to cry.
The bartender-- gosh, what was his name?
He comes over and hugs us both because he was a very good Polish national.
He risked his life coming to the ghetto.
He was helping out a lot.
And of course, he was making little money, too, but he was a OK guy.
And it was my reunion with my brother.
And come to find out, I haven't seen him since that assembly place.
He was shipped to Auschwitz.
He was hiding out for about six weeks.
They finally caught him and sent him to Auschwitz.
And in Auschwitz, he saved his life.
As they were unloading, he saw a doctor--
Yeah.
Right.
